text: The surgeon has opened the abdomen and mobilized the liver, removing the gallbladder. The junction of the right and left liver is visible through the gallbladder fossa, indicated by the surgeon's forceps. The area to the right of the forceps will be resected, while the tissue under the retractor on the left will remain.
The surgeon has already ligated the right hepatic artery, which is located beneath the cystic duct stump. The dual blood supply to the liver is being addressed during the resection.
The surgeon prepares to take the right branch of the portal vein, which is visible underneath the liver tissue. The surgical field is clearly exposed for visualization.
The surgeon places a surgical stapler across the portal vein to aid in the resection process. The stapler is positioned carefully to ensure proper closure of the vein.
The surgeon displays the stapled and cut right portal vein, highlighting the area where sutures could potentially kink the vessel and cause stenosis.

The main portal vein and left portal vein are visible, with the liver showing signs of demarcation. The right side of the liver appears darker, indicating the inflow has been cut off, while the area where the gallbladder was removed is also visible. The normal liver color is lighter on the left side.

The surgeon has mobilized the liver off the inferior vena cava (IVC) up to the right hepatic vein. The liver appears normal with healthy tissue, despite the patient having undergone chemotherapy. The remnant liver is visually healthy, and the surgical team is prepared to proceed through the liver without taking the right hepatic vein outside.

The surgeon cauterizes the area around the old ligamentum teres, preparing to enter the ischemic part of the liver using RF devices. The RF devices are used to coagulate tissue and occlude vessels smaller than three millimeters, while larger vessels may bleed.
